programs.name: Stay NJ
programs.tagline: If your parent is 65+ and owns their New Jersey home, they may qualify for up to $6,500 back on property taxes.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and owns their New Jersey home, Stay NJ can reimburse 50% of their property taxes — up to $6,500 per year. Because the program reimburses actual property taxes paid, parents with higher tax bills get larger benefits (those paying $13,000+ in property taxes receive the maximum $6,500).

Your parent must have owned and lived in their New Jersey home for the full prior year and have annual income below $500,000. The program covers traditional homeowners paying property taxes or P.I.L.O.T. payments, but mobile homeowners don't qualify. One application (PAS-1) automatically checks eligibility for Stay NJ plus other New Jersey property tax relief programs.
programs.savingsSource: 50% reimbursement of property taxes up to maximum $6,500

summary: Lived in home full prior year
otherRequirements: Must own and live in New Jersey home as principal residence for full 12 months of prior year
Must pay property taxes (mobile homeowners not eligible)
P.I.L.O.T. payments qualify

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at propertytaxreliefapp.nj.gov or mail the PAS-1 form by October 31.

steps.title: Gather documents
steps.description: Latest property tax bill and gross income from most recent state tax return

steps.title: Choose application method
steps.description: Online requires ID.me verification with driver's license, state ID, or passport. Mail option: print and send PAS-1 form

steps.title: Complete PAS-1 application
steps.description: Single form automatically checks eligibility for Stay NJ, ANCHOR, and Senior Freeze programs

steps.title: Submit by deadline
steps.description: Applications due October 31. Online required for direct deposit of reimbursement

applicationGuide.tip: Apply online if possible — it's required for direct deposit and processes faster than mail applications.

categories.name: Identity
items: Driver's license, state ID, passport card, or passport (for online application only)

categories.name: Property
items: Property information from state Tax Assessor's office (if no tax bill available)

categories.name: Financial
items: Gross income from most recent New Jersey state income tax return

contentSections.text: The October 31 deadline is firm. If your parent misses it, they'll have to wait until the next application cycle for the following tax year.

faqs.question: How much will my parent actually get back?
faqs.answer: Your parent gets 50% of their property taxes back, up to $6,500 maximum. If they pay $8,000 in property taxes, they get $4,000. If they pay $15,000, they get the maximum $6,500.

faqs.question: What if my parent just moved to their current home?
faqs.answer: They must have owned and lived in the home for the full 12 months of the prior year. If they moved in 2025, they can't apply until 2027 (for 2026 taxes).

faqs.question: Can my parent get Stay NJ if they live in a mobile home?
faqs.answer: No. Mobile homeowners are explicitly not eligible for Stay NJ, even if they pay property taxes or site fees.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is from Social Security disability?
faqs.answer: Social Security disability income counts toward the $500,000 limit, but disability status alone doesn't qualify them for Stay NJ — they must still be 65+ and meet all other requirements.

faqs.question: Will applying for Stay NJ affect other benefits my parent receives?
faqs.answer: No. Stay NJ is a property tax reimbursement that doesn't count as income for other programs like SNAP or Medicaid.

faqs.question: What happens if the state determines my parent qualifies for multiple programs?
faqs.answer: The PAS-1 application automatically checks eligibility for Stay NJ, ANCHOR, and Senior Freeze. Your parent will receive whichever program provides the highest benefit — they can't combine programs.

programs.name: Senior Freeze (Property Tax Reimbursement)
programs.tagline: Get reimbursed for property tax increases if your parent is 65+ and has lived in their New Jersey home for years.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and owns their New Jersey home, they may qualify to get money back when property taxes increase. Senior Freeze reimburses the difference between current property taxes and what they paid in their first qualifying year (called the "base year"). This isn't a tax freeze — taxes still go up, but the state pays the increase.

Your parent must have lived in New Jersey continuously for 10 years and owned their home since their base year. Income limit: $99,735 or less per year for recent applications (limits updated annually). They must meet all requirements every single year from their base year through the application year to remain eligible.
programs.savingsSource: Reimbursement varies by property tax increases

summary: Own and live in home since base year
otherRequirements: Lived in New Jersey continuously for 10 years before base year
Owned and lived in same home since base year through application year
Property taxes paid by June 1 each year (homeowners) or site fees by December 31 (mobile home owners)
Must meet all requirements every year from base year to application year

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through the combined PAS-1 application, which also covers ANCHOR and Stay NJ programs.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of age, 10 years of New Jersey residency, home ownership documents, and property tax payment receipts for all relevant years.

steps.title: Complete PAS-1 application online
steps.description: Use the combined application at nj.gov/treasury/taxation/ptr/ — this single form covers Senior Freeze, ANCHOR, and Stay NJ programs.

steps.title: Submit income documentation
steps.description: Provide income records for all years from your parent's base year through the application year.

steps.title: Wait for state determination
steps.description: New Jersey will send a letter explaining which programs your parent qualifies for and the reimbursement amount.

applicationGuide.tip: Don't try to calculate the reimbursement yourself — the state determines eligibility and amount after reviewing your application.

contentSections.text: Your parent must meet ALL requirements every single year from their base year to the application year. Missing eligibility for even one year disqualifies them permanently.

faqs.question: What if Mom's property taxes went down instead of up?
faqs.answer: She won't receive any reimbursement that year since Senior Freeze only pays for increases above the base year amount. But she can still apply to maintain eligibility for future years when taxes might increase.

faqs.question: Can Dad still get Senior Freeze if he moves to a different house in New Jersey?
faqs.answer: Only if he received reimbursement for his previous residence in the last full year he lived there. Otherwise, moving breaks the continuous ownership requirement and he loses eligibility.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom was eligible for three years but missed applying one year?
faqs.answer: She loses Senior Freeze eligibility permanently. The program requires meeting all requirements every single year from the base year through the application year with no gaps.

faqs.question: Does the income limit include both parents if they're married?
faqs.answer: Yes. If your parents are married or in a civil union and living in the same home, their combined income must be under the limit ($99,735 for recent applications).

faqs.question: When is the application deadline?
faqs.answer: Deadlines are strict and vary by year. For example, 2025 applications are due by November 2, 2026. Check the current deadline at nj.gov/treasury/taxation/ptr/ since late applications aren't accepted.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Senior Freeze and ANCHOR?
faqs.answer: Senior Freeze reimburses property tax increases above your parent's base year amount. ANCHOR provides a flat benefit amount. The same PAS-1 application covers both — New Jersey determines which programs your parent qualifies for.

programs.name: Jersey Assistance for Community Caregiving (JACC)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home-level care but earns too much for Medicaid, JACC provides up to $1,156/month in home care services.

localEntities.name: County Aging and Disability Resource Connections via 1-877-222-3737
Examples: Hunterdon Human Services (908-788-1361), Warren Division of Aging (908-475-6591), FCS Monmouth/Mercer/Ocean[2][3][7]

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication but earns too much to qualify for Medicaid, JACC bridges this gap. The program provides up to $1,156 per month in home care services — including personal care, homemaking, and care coordination — for New Jersey residents age 60+ who need nursing facility level of care but want to stay home.

Because JACC serves people who fall in the coverage gap (too much income for Medicaid, not enough for private care), it has higher income limits — up to 365% of Federal Poverty Level. Your parent pays a sliding-scale co-pay based on their income, but the bulk of services are covered.

The program also allows your parent to hire family members or friends as paid caregivers through the Participant-Employed Providers option, giving you more control over who provides care.

summary: Income up to 365% Federal Poverty Level
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be determined clinically eligible for nursing facility level of care (needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management)
otherRequirements: New Jersey resident
Lives in own home or with family/friends (not in licensed facility)
US citizen or Qualified Alien
Not receiving other state-funded programs

applicationGuide.summary: Call 1-877-222-3737 for NJ EASE to start the application and clinical assessment process.

steps.title: Call NJ EASE
steps.description: Call 1-877-222-3737 to begin the application process and schedule a clinical assessment.

steps.title: Complete clinical assessment
steps.description: A care manager will assess your parent's need for nursing facility level of care and determine eligibility.

steps.title: Financial review
steps.description: Provide income, asset, and residency documentation for financial eligibility determination.

steps.title: Develop Plan of Care
steps.description: If eligible, work with care manager to create a personalized Plan of Care based on your parent's specific needs and monthly service cap.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Subject to funding availability — waitlists may exist
applicationGuide.tip: Have your parent's income, asset, and medical information ready when you call to speed up the process.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Countable resources must be $40,000 or less for individual
Countable resources must be $60,000 or less for couple
excluded: Specific exempt assets not detailed in available sources

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of US citizenship or Qualified Alien status

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records showing need for daily assistance

contentSections.text: JACC has monthly service caps around $1,100-$1,156 per person and is subject to funding availability. Waitlists may exist.
JACC allows your parent to hire family members or friends as paid caregivers through the Participant-Employed Providers option — this gives you more control over care.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and savings if they qualify for JACC?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent can own their home and have up to $40,000 in countable assets ($60,000 for couples). Because JACC isn't Medicaid, there are no estate recovery requirements.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the Medicaid limit but they need help at home?
faqs.answer: JACC is designed exactly for this situation. It serves people who earn too much for Medicaid but still need nursing home-level care. Income limits go up to 365% of Federal Poverty Level, much higher than Medicaid.

faqs.question: Can I get paid to care for my parent through JACC?
faqs.answer: Yes. JACC's Participant-Employed Providers option allows your parent to hire family members or friends as paid caregivers, giving you more control over who provides care.

faqs.question: How much will my parent have to pay out-of-pocket?
faqs.answer: Your parent pays a sliding-scale co-pay based on their countable income. The exact amount depends on their financial situation, but the bulk of services up to the monthly cap are covered.

faqs.question: What's the difference between JACC and Medicaid home care?
faqs.answer: JACC serves people who don't qualify for Medicaid due to higher income or assets. It has higher income limits (365% Federal Poverty Level) but caps services around $1,156/month, while Medicaid may provide more extensive services.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent needs more care than the monthly cap allows?
faqs.answer: JACC caps services around $1,156/month. If your parent needs more intensive care, you'd need to pay privately for additional services or explore other options like transitioning to Medicaid if their financial situation changes.

programs.name: Pharmaceutical Assistance to the Aged & Disabled (PAAD)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 65+ and earns less than $54,943/year, they can get prescriptions for just $5-7 per medication.

localEntities.name: Central processing in Trenton; pharmacies statewide accept PAAD

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ (or disabled and receiving Social Security disability benefits) with income under $54,943/year (single) or $62,390/year (married), PAAD can reduce their prescription costs to just $5 for generic drugs and $7 for brand-name drugs — no matter how expensive the medication actually costs.

Because PAAD works with Medicare Part D, your parent must enroll in a Medicare prescription drug plan, but PAAD will pay the premium for qualifying low-cost plans. This means they get both Medicare Part D coverage and the reduced copays through PAAD.

Unlike Medicaid, PAAD has no asset limits — your parent can own a home, have savings, and still qualify based solely on income. The program covers all FDA-approved prescription drugs and insulin, though it doesn't cover diabetic testing supplies.
programs.savingsSource: Based on $5-7 copays versus typical prescription costs for seniors taking multiple medications

summary: Must enroll in Medicare Part D
otherRequirements: New Jersey resident
Not eligible for Medicaid
Must enroll in Medicare Part D prescription drug plan
U.S. citizenship not required

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at njsaves.org — processing time not specified by the state.

steps.title: Enroll in Medicare Part D first
steps.description: If your parent has Medicare, they must enroll in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan before applying to PAAD. PAAD will pay the premium for qualifying low-cost plans.

steps.title: Complete form AP-2 online
steps.description: Go to njsaves.org and click 'Start New Application.' Fill out the Universal Application for PAAD (form AP-2).

steps.title: Upload required documents
steps.description: Provide proof of income (tax returns, Social Security statements), proof of New Jersey residency, Medicare cards, and proof of disability if under 65.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Applications are processed centrally in Trenton. You'll receive notification of approval and can use PAAD at participating pharmacies statewide.

applicationGuide.tip: Income limits change annually — use 2026 figures ($54,943 single/$62,390 married) when applying in 2026.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits. Updated annually by federal guidelines.

categories.name: Residency & Identity
items: Medicare cards (Parts A, B, and D if applicable)
Proof of Social Security Title II Disability (if under 65)

categories.name: Medical Coverage
items: Documentation showing not eligible for Medicaid

contentSections.text: Your parent must enroll in Medicare Part D even to get PAAD — but choose carefully. PAAD only pays premiums for specific low-cost plans at or near the regional benchmark. Self-enrolling in a higher-premium plan may disqualify them from premium assistance.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house and savings if she qualifies for PAAD?
faqs.answer: Yes. PAAD has no asset limits — your mom can own a home, have savings in the bank, and still qualify based solely on her income being under the limits.

faqs.question: What if Dad already has Medicare Part D but it's expensive?
faqs.answer: PAAD will pay the premium only for specific low-cost plans at or near the regional benchmark. If his current plan is more expensive, he may need to switch to a qualifying plan to get premium assistance, but he'll still get the $5-7 copays regardless.

faqs.question: Does PAAD cover diabetic testing supplies?
faqs.answer: No. PAAD covers insulin and insulin supplies, plus syringes and needles for MS injectables, but does not cover diabetic testing supplies or strips.

faqs.question: What's the difference between PAAD and Senior Gold?
faqs.answer: PAAD is for lower incomes (under $54,943 single) with $5-7 copays. Senior Gold is for higher incomes with different copay structures. Don't apply for both — choose based on your parent's income level.

faqs.question: Can Mom use PAAD if she moves from another state?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as she becomes a New Jersey resident. U.S. citizenship is not required — lawful permanent residents and other legal residents can qualify.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the PAAD limit?
faqs.answer: She may qualify for Senior Gold, which has higher income limits but different copay amounts. Check both programs to see which fits her situation better.

programs.name: Lifeline Credit
programs.tagline: If your parent is 65+ and on a fixed income, they may qualify for $225/year off their electric and gas bills in New Jersey.
programs.intro:
If your parent is struggling with utility bills in New Jersey, Lifeline Credit provides up to $225 per year directly credited to their electric and natural gas bills. This program specifically helps seniors 65+ and disabled adults with limited incomes — but only covers electric and natural gas customers (not oil, propane, or other heating sources).

The credit appears automatically on their utility bill once approved. Because only one person per household can receive this benefit, you'll need to decide which family member should apply if multiple people are eligible. Income limits are $54,943/year for single seniors or $62,390/year for married couples in 2026.
programs.savingsSource: Annual bill credit for eligible electric and natural gas customers

summary: Electric or natural gas customer only
otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in or eligible for PAAD (Pharmaceutical Assistance to the Aged and Disabled)
Must be residential electric or natural gas customer only
Only one applicant per household
U.S. citizenship not required

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through the NJSave application, which covers both PAAD and Lifeline Credit in one form.

steps.title: Complete NJSave application online
steps.description: The application automatically includes Lifeline Credit questions when you apply for PAAD eligibility.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need proof of income for past 30 days, Social Security cards, photo ID, and most recent utility bill.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: If your parent receives SSI, they get Lifeline Credit automatically in their check — do not apply separately.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent already receives MAA, MAO, or New Jersey Care benefits, they'll automatically get a Lifeline application mailed to them each August.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits (2026)
contentSections.footnote: Income limits are individual gross income for the applicant, not total household income beyond spouse

categories.name: Identity
items: Photo ID (driver's license, passport, or government-issued ID)
Social Security cards for household members

categories.name: Financial
items: Proof of income for past 30 days
Proof of address (rental lease, mortgage statement, tax bill, or deed)

contentSections.text: This program only covers electric and natural gas — not oil, propane, wood, or coal heating. If your parent uses these other heating sources, they won't qualify.

faqs.question: What if my parent gets SSI?
faqs.answer: If your parent receives SSI, they automatically get the Lifeline Credit included in their SSI check. Do not apply separately — the credit is handled automatically.

faqs.question: Can both my parents apply if they're married?
faqs.answer: No, only one person per household can receive Lifeline Credit. If both parents are eligible, choose one to apply. The benefit covers the household's electric and gas bills regardless of whose name is on the application.

faqs.question: What if my parent heats with oil or propane?
faqs.answer: They won't qualify for Lifeline Credit. This program only covers electric and natural gas customers. Oil, propane, wood, and coal heating are specifically excluded.

faqs.question: How does the $225 credit appear on the bill?
faqs.answer: The credit appears directly on your parent's utility bill as an annual credit. They don't receive a separate check — the utility company applies the credit to reduce what they owe.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits are firm — $54,943/year for single seniors or $62,390/year for married couples in 2026. If your parent's gross income exceeds these amounts, they won't qualify for Lifeline Credit.

faqs.question: Does my parent need to apply every year?
faqs.answer: If your parent receives MAA, MAO, or New Jersey Care benefits, applications are automatically mailed each August. For others, eligibility is checked as of July 1 or within the next 6 months, but the research doesn't specify annual reapplication requirements.

programs.name: New Jersey Statewide Respite Care Program
programs.tagline: Get temporary caregiver relief through in-home care, adult day programs, or short-term placement for your parent.
programs.intro: If you're caring for a parent with a chronic disability or illness, New Jersey's Statewide Respite Care Program provides temporary relief through professional caregivers. The program offers companion care, home health aides, adult day programs, and even short-term placement in approved homes or medical facilities. Your parent pays 0-25% of the service cost based on their income — many families pay nothing. To qualify, your parent must live at home (not in a facility), have income below $2,982/month if single ($5,964 if married), and receive daily care from you or another unpaid family member. A licensed medical provider must certify that your parent needs supervision or assistance with daily activities.
programs.savingsSource: Subsidized service with sliding scale co-pay

summary: Lives at home with unpaid caregiver

countedAssets: Assets that can be converted to cash within 20 working days

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must have a chronic physical or mental disability requiring supervision or assistance with basic daily needs, certified by a licensed medical provider
otherRequirements: Must live in New Jersey
Must live in the community (not in assisted living, nursing home, or residential care facility)
Must receive daily care from an unpaid caregiver age 18 or older
Cannot participate in MLTSS, JACC, Alzheimer's Adult Day Services, or Congregate Housing Services programs

applicationGuide.summary: Call the Aging and Disability Resource Connection at 1-877-222-3737 to start your application.

steps.title: Call ADRC
steps.description: Contact 1-877-222-3737 to discuss your parent's needs and begin the eligibility assessment.

steps.title: Income and asset verification
steps.description: Provide documentation of your parent's income and assets to confirm financial eligibility.

steps.title: Medical certification
steps.description: Have your parent's licensed medical provider certify their functional impairments and need for daily supervision or assistance.

steps.title: County assignment
steps.description: ADRC will connect you with your county's local sponsor agency that administers the program.

applicationGuide.tip: Priority goes to families where the care recipient is at risk of nursing home placement due to caregiver burnout — mention this if it applies to your situation.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Any assets convertible to cash within 20 working days

contentSections.text: Only your parent's income counts for eligibility — your income as the caregiver doesn't matter, even if you live in the same household.
You cannot receive services from this program if your parent is already enrolled in MLTSS, JACC, Alzheimer's Adult Day Services, or Congregate Housing Services programs.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the $2,982 limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits are strict. If your parent exceeds $2,982/month (single) or $5,964/month (married), they won't qualify. However, these limits are updated annually, and only your parent's income counts — not yours.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep getting respite care if she moves to assisted living?
faqs.answer: No. This program is only for people living in the community. If your parent moves to assisted living, a nursing home, or any residential care facility, they lose eligibility.

faqs.question: How much will we actually pay for services?
faqs.answer: You'll pay 0-25% of the service cost based on your parent's income. Many families with lower incomes pay nothing. The exact percentage depends on where your parent's income falls on the sliding scale.

faqs.question: What happens if I can no longer provide daily care?
faqs.answer: Your parent must receive daily care from an unpaid caregiver to qualify. If you can no longer provide this care and no other unpaid family member or friend can take over, your parent may lose eligibility for this program.

faqs.question: Can we use respite care for emergencies?
faqs.answer: Yes. Once enrolled, you get access to emergency respite care as well as planned respite services. The program recognizes that caregiving crises happen unexpectedly.

faqs.question: What if our county's program has different rules?
faqs.answer: While each county administers the program locally, the eligibility requirements and basic structure are the same statewide. However, available services or wait times may vary by county.

programs.name: Alzheimer's Adult Day Services Program
programs.tagline: Subsidized dementia-specialized day programs help your parent stay engaged while giving you daily caregiver relief.
programs.intro:
If your parent has been diagnosed with Alzheimer's or another dementia and you're providing daily care, this program subsidizes their attendance at specialized adult day centers throughout New Jersey. Because these centers are designed specifically for people with cognitive loss, your parent gets appropriate activities and high staff-to-client ratios while you get respite during the day.

The program covers attendance at both social model and medical model adult day centers, all with dementia specialization. You'll pay an income-based copay rather than full private-pay rates. The program serves people at any stage of dementia as long as family or friends are providing daily assistance.

Your parent must have a diagnosis of permanent, progressive dementia and be a New Jersey resident. They cannot currently be receiving Medicaid long-term services (MLTSS), JACC, or Congregate Housing Services.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need daily assistance from family or friends with care due to dementia symptoms
otherRequirements: New Jersey resident

Diagnosis of qualifying dementia (Alzheimer's, Pick's disease, Parkinson's dementia, Huntington's dementia, vascular dementia, Creutzfeldt-Jakob, Fronto-Temporal, Lewy body, normal pressure hydrocephalus dementia, or Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome)
Not currently participating in Medicaid MLTSS, JACC, or Congregate Housing Services Program

applicationGuide.summary: Find a participating center online, then contact them directly to apply and get current income guidelines.

steps.title: Find participating centers
steps.description: Use the center finder at nj.gov/humanservices to locate dementia-specialized adult day centers near you that accept this program.

steps.title: Contact center directly
steps.description: Call your chosen center to get current income and asset guidelines, since specific dollar amounts aren't published in state materials.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of NJ residency, medical diagnosis of qualifying dementia, income documentation, and asset information.

steps.title: Apply through center or Division of Aging Services
steps.description: Submit your application either directly to the participating center or through your local Division of Aging Services office.

applicationGuide.tip: Since exact income limits aren't published, call centers directly before gathering all documents to confirm your parent likely qualifies.

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical diagnosis of qualifying dementia from licensed provider
Documentation of functional impairments requiring daily assistance

categories.name: Financial
items: Income documentation for all household members

contentSections.text: You cannot use this program if your parent is already receiving Medicaid long-term services (MLTSS). The program is designed as an alternative for families not yet using Medicaid.

faqs.question: What if my parent is already on Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Your parent cannot participate if they're currently receiving NJ FamilyCare (Medicaid), Managed Long-Term Services and Supports (MLTSS), JACC, or Congregate Housing Services. This program serves as an alternative for families not yet using these other programs.

faqs.question: Can any adult day center accept this program?
faqs.answer: No. Only participating centers that specialize in dementia care accept this program. These centers have higher staff-to-client ratios and activities designed for people with cognitive loss. Use the state's center finder to locate qualifying providers.

faqs.question: What dementia diagnoses qualify?
faqs.answer: Qualifying diagnoses include Alzheimer's disease, Pick's disease, dementia due to Parkinson's, Huntington's, vascular/multi-infarct dementia, Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, Fronto-Temporal dementia, Lewy body dementia, normal pressure hydrocephalus dementia, or Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome. The diagnosis must be permanent and progressive.

faqs.question: How much will we pay out of pocket?
faqs.answer: You'll pay an income-based copay, but the state doesn't publish specific dollar amounts. Contact participating centers directly to get current copay rates based on your family's income and household size.

faqs.question: What if my parent is in early-stage dementia?
faqs.answer: The program serves people at any stage of dementia, as long as family or friends are providing daily assistance. Early-stage participants often benefit from the specialized programming and social interaction.

faqs.question: Where can I find the exact income limits?
faqs.answer: The state requires documented evidence of income eligibility but doesn't publish specific dollar thresholds. Contact the Division of Aging Services or participating centers directly to get current income and asset guidelines for your household size.

programs.name: Senior Citizen E-ZPass Discount
programs.tagline: If your parent drives regularly on NJ Turnpike or Parkway, they can save 10% on tolls with this age 65+ discount.

localEntities.name: Delaware River Port Authority (DRPA) for bridges

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and drives regularly on the New Jersey Turnpike or Garden State Parkway, they can get a 10% discount on off-peak tolls. The program requires a New Jersey E-ZPass account (anyone can open one, regardless of state residency) and your parent must be the actual driver when the discount is used.

There are no income limits — eligibility is based only on age 65+, having a valid driver's license, and a passenger vehicle registered in their name or their spouse's name. The discount applies during weekdays off-peak hours and all weekend hours on the Turnpike and Parkway. A separate program covers four bridges between New Jersey and Pennsylvania.

Your parent must renew annually, and the renewal date aligns with their vehicle registration renewal. Only one E-ZPass tag per senior is allowed, and they must be physically driving (not just a passenger) for the discount to apply.

otherRequirements: Must have active New Jersey E-ZPass account
Must have valid driver's license from any state
Must have passenger vehicle registered in their name or spouse's name
Must be driving (not passenger) when using discount
Business/commercial accounts not eligible

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at the NJTA website with your E-ZPass account number and required documents — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Get New Jersey E-ZPass account
steps.description: If your parent doesn't have one, open a NJ E-ZPass account online at ezpassnj.com or call 888-288-6865. Out-of-state residents can open NJ accounts.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need: copy of driver's license (to prove age 65+), copy of vehicle registration in their name or spouse's name, NJ E-ZPass account number (13 digits), and E-ZPass tag number (11 digits).

steps.title: Apply for Turnpike/Parkway discount
steps.description: Complete application online at seniordiscount.njta.gov or call 732-750-5300. For DRPA bridges, call 856-968-2000 press 5 separately.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent uses the four bridges to Pennsylvania frequently, apply for the separate DRPA program which gives $18 credit for 18+ trips per month.

categories.name: Identity & Age Verification
items: Copy of valid driver's license (must show age 65+ — no other ID accepted)

categories.name: Vehicle & Account Information
items: Copy of passenger vehicle registration (in your parent's name or spouse's name)
If leasing: Top portion of lease agreement showing your parent as lessee

contentSections.text: Your parent must be the actual driver to get the discount — if someone else drives their car, the discount won't apply and could result in program removal.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives in Pennsylvania but drives in New Jersey frequently?
faqs.answer: They can open a New Jersey E-ZPass account regardless of where they live. The vehicle just needs to be registered in their name or their spouse's name in any state.

faqs.question: Can my parent use the discount if I'm driving their car?
faqs.answer: No. Your parent must be the driver when the toll is charged. If others use their E-ZPass tag, it could result in removal from the program.

faqs.question: Does this work during rush hour?
faqs.answer: No. The 10% discount only applies during off-peak hours on weekdays and all hours on weekends. Peak hour tolls get no discount.

faqs.question: What's the difference between the NJTA program and DRPA program?
faqs.answer: NJTA covers Turnpike and Parkway with 10% off-peak discount. DRPA covers four specific bridges (Benjamin Franklin, Walt Whitman, Betsy Ross, Commodore Barry) and gives $18 credit if you make 18+ trips in a month. You need to apply separately for each.

faqs.question: How often does my parent need to renew?
faqs.answer: Annually, and the renewal date matches their vehicle registration renewal date. So their first enrollment period might be less than 12 months depending on when they apply.

faqs.question: Can my parent have multiple E-ZPass tags for different vehicles?
faqs.answer: No. Only one E-ZPass tag per senior is allowed for the discount program, and it must start with 009 or 022.

stateOverview.intro:
New Jersey offers 8 senior benefit programs that could save your parent thousands annually — from $6,500 property tax rebates to $5 prescription medications. Most focus on financial relief: property tax assistance, prescription drug savings, utility bill reductions, and subsidized care services. Unlike many states that primarily offer information and referrals, New Jersey provides direct financial benefits if your parent qualifies based on age, income, or care needs.

Start by checking the highest-value programs first: Stay NJ for property tax relief, PAAD for prescription savings, and JACC if your parent needs home care but earns too much for Medicaid. Most have income limits, but several programs serve middle-income seniors who wouldn't qualify for federal assistance.

startHere.name: Stay NJ
startHere.why: Provides the largest potential savings — up to $6,500 back on property taxes for homeowners 65+.

startHere.name: PAAD
startHere.why: Reduces prescription costs to just $5-7 per medication for seniors earning less than $54,943/year.

startHere.name: JACC
startHere.why: Provides up to $1,156/month in home care for parents who need nursing home-level care but earn too much for Medicaid.

startHere.name: Lifeline Credit
startHere.why: Quick $225/year savings on utility bills for seniors on fixed incomes.

byNeed.description: Two separate programs that can reduce or reimburse property taxes for homeowners 65+.
State program that caps prescription costs at $5-7 per medication for qualifying seniors.
Monthly funding for in-home care when your parent needs nursing home-level assistance.

programs: New Jersey Statewide Respite Care Program
byNeed.description: Temporary care services and specialized day programs to give family caregivers breaks.
Ongoing discounts on utilities and highway tolls for qualifying seniors.

quickFacts: 7 out of 8 New Jersey senior programs provide direct financial benefits, not just information or referrals
Income limits vary widely: PAAD allows up to $54,943/year while other programs have much lower thresholds
Property owners have two separate tax relief options: Stay NJ (rebates) and Senior Freeze (reimbursement for increases)
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Most New Jersey senior programs are qualification-based benefits requiring age, income, or asset verification — like PAAD's prescription drug savings or Stay NJ's property tax rebates. Only the Senior Freeze program functions as a free resource, providing reimbursement information to homeowners without strict financial qualifying criteria. The majority require applications and annual income documentation to receive direct financial assistance.
